module: Module 3 - Compute Options, Serverless, and Containers

sections.title: Unmanaged vs Managed vs Serverless
points: Unmanaged services like EC2 give you full control but require you to manage OS, scaling, and maintenance.
Managed services reduce operational work by letting AWS handle infrastructure tasks.
Serverless services remove the need to manage servers entirely.
In serverless, AWS handles provisioning, scaling, availability, and maintenance.
You still remain responsible for your application code and permissions.
sections.exam_tip: More control = more responsibility. Less management = more AWS responsibility.

sections.title: Amazon EC2 (Unmanaged)
points: EC2 requires you to manage OS, patches, scaling, and configuration.
AWS handles hardware, networking, and data centers.
EC2 provides maximum flexibility and customization.
You are responsible for security IN the cloud.
sections.exam_tip: If you control OS and software → EC2 (unmanaged).

sections.title: Managed Services
points: Managed services reduce infrastructure management.
AWS handles scaling, availability, and maintenance.
Examples include ELB, SQS, SNS.
You configure the service, AWS runs it.
sections.exam_tip: If AWS runs the infrastructure for you → managed service.

sections.title: Serverless Computing
points: Serverless means you do not manage servers at all.
AWS automatically handles scaling and infrastructure.
You only focus on code execution.
You pay only for usage, not idle resources.
sections.exam_tip: No servers to manage + pay per use → serverless.

sections.title: AWS Lambda Basics
points: Lambda is a serverless compute service.
It runs code in response to events.
No need to provision or manage servers.
Lambda automatically scales based on demand.
You are billed based on execution time (milliseconds).

sections.title: Lambda Key Features
points: Maximum execution time is 15 minutes.
Supports multiple programming languages like Python, Java, Node.js.
Uses runtimes to execute code.
Integrates easily with other AWS services.
Highly scalable and automatically adjusts to demand.
sections.exam_tip: If execution time is limited → Lambda might not be ideal.

sections.title: Lambda Triggers
points: Lambda runs when triggered by an event.
Triggers include S3 uploads, SQS messages, API calls, or events.
Example: image upload triggers image processing.
Example: message in queue triggers processing.

sections.title: Lambda Real-World Use Cases
points: Image processing when users upload files.
Real-time data processing.
Event-driven workflows.
Backend logic for web applications.
Automated responses to system events.
sections.exam_tip: If work only happens when something happens → Lambda.

sections.title: Lambda Responsibility Model
points: AWS manages infrastructure, scaling, and availability.
Customer manages code and permissions.
Customer is responsible for access control and data security.
No need to patch OS or manage servers.
sections.exam_tip: Lambda = you manage code, AWS manages everything else.

sections.title: Containers Overview
points: Containers package code, runtime, dependencies, and configuration.
They provide consistent environments across systems.
Containers solve 'it works on my machine' problems.
Containers are lightweight and fast to start.

sections.title: Containers vs Virtual Machines
points: Containers share the host OS and are lightweight.
Virtual machines run full OS and are heavier.
Containers start faster and use fewer resources.
VMs provide stronger isolation but more overhead.

sections.title: Container Orchestration
points: Orchestration manages container lifecycle.
Handles scaling, deployment, monitoring, and recovery.
Automatically adjusts resources based on demand.
Essential for large-scale container environments.

sections.title: Amazon ECS
points: Amazon ECS is a container orchestration service.
It is AWS-native and easier to use than Kubernetes.
Manages container deployment and scaling.
Works with EC2 or Fargate.

sections.title: Amazon EKS
points: Amazon EKS runs Kubernetes on AWS.
Provides more flexibility and control.
Used for complex or enterprise workloads.
Supports open-source Kubernetes ecosystem.

sections.title: Amazon ECR
points: ECR is a container image registry.
Stores and manages container images.
Used to push and pull container images.
Works with ECS and EKS.

sections.title: AWS Fargate
points: Fargate is serverless compute for containers.
No need to manage servers.
Works with ECS and EKS.
You pay only for resources used by containers.

sections.title: EC2 vs Fargate for Containers
points: EC2 gives full control over infrastructure.
Fargate removes infrastructure management.
EC2 requires patching and scaling management.
Fargate is easier but less customizable.

sections.title: Elastic Beanstalk
points: Elastic Beanstalk is a fully managed application deployment service.
You upload code and AWS handles infrastructure.
Automatically manages scaling, load balancing, and monitoring.
Still provides access to underlying resources.
sections.exam_tip: Deploy app without managing infra → Elastic Beanstalk.

sections.title: AWS Batch
points: AWS Batch is used for large-scale batch processing.
Automatically provisions and scales compute resources.
Ideal for data processing, simulations, and analytics.
Optimizes resource usage based on job requirements.

sections.title: Amazon Lightsail
points: Lightsail provides simple VPS-style hosting.
Offers predictable monthly pricing.
Good for small apps, websites, and beginners.
Simplifies AWS complexity.

sections.title: AWS Outposts
points: Outposts extends AWS infrastructure to on-premises.
Supports hybrid cloud environments.
Used for low latency or compliance requirements.
Provides consistent AWS experience locally.

sections.title: Choosing the Right Compute Service
points: EC2 → full control and customization.
Lambda → event-driven serverless.
ECS/EKS → container orchestration.
Fargate → serverless containers.
Elastic Beanstalk → easy app deployment.
Batch → large processing jobs.
Lightsail → simple hosting.
Outposts → hybrid environments.
sections.exam_tip: Always match the service to the workload requirement.

sections.title: Fast Memory Anchors
points: EC2 = full control.
Lambda = serverless + events.
ECS = containers (simple).
EKS = Kubernetes.
Fargate = serverless containers.
ECR = container storage.
Beanstalk = deploy apps easily.
Batch = heavy jobs.
Lightsail = simple VPS.
Outposts = hybrid cloud.
sections.exam_tip: Memorize this block → covers most exam questions.

sections.title: Common Exam Traps
points: Lambda is not for long-running tasks (15 min limit).
ECS and EKS are orchestration, not compute engines.
Fargate is compute, not orchestration.
ECR stores images, it does not run containers.
Beanstalk is not serverless—it still uses EC2 behind the scenes.
sections.exam_tip: AWS loves mixing these services in questions.
